preFix.title: Using a broody hen to hatch eggs
preFix.subtitle: Natural incubation with no electricity and a better hatch rate.
preFix.excerpt: A committed broody hen runs at 37.5°C, turns her eggs every hour, adjusts humidity by feel, and does not experience power cuts. How to confirm she is ready, set her up correctly, manage the 21 days, and handle hatch day.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, brooding chapter. Cornell Small Farms Programme, hatching eggs naturally. University of Wisconsin Extension, backyard poultry series.

content.text: A
broody hen

is a better incubator than most machines. She turns each egg up to an hour, adjusts her body position to control humidity as the hatch approaches, and keeps the temperature at a steady 37.5°C with no thermostat drift. The tutorial covers confirming she is committed, giving her the right setup, managing the 21 days, and what to do when the first chicks begin to
pip
Is the hen really broody?

A hen that simply visits the nest box to lay and leaves is not a broody. A hen that visits and stays, spreading herself low over the eggs and puffing up aggressively when disturbed, is. Test her twice: approach the nest and put a hand under her chest. A committed broody will lower herself further, cluck sharply, and peck the back of your hand. A hen that stands and moves off is not ready. Check again the following morning.

Some hens go broody strongly from the first day; others take two or three days to commit fully. Set the hatching eggs only when she is sitting tight around the clock and leaving the nest once a day at most.
Setting her up

Move the broody and her nest to a separate, quiet space away from the main flock. Other hens jumping into and out of the nest box to lay disrupts incubation and can break eggs. A dedicated broody coop or a partitioned corner of the shed is ideal.
Place the
clutch

of hatching eggs in the nest after dark or early morning when the hen is calm. Slide the eggs under her one at a time; do not replace any dummy eggs until all the hatching eggs are in place. A Light Sussex or Silkie can cover 10 to 12 standard eggs; a smaller breed covers 6 to 8. Mark each egg with a pencil cross so you can check whether she is turning them.
Set food and water within reach of the nest but not so close that she fouls them. She will leave to eat, drink, and dust-bathe once a day; this 20-minute break is normal and does not harm the eggs.
The 21 days
Disturb the hen as little as possible once she is set. Check food and water daily. On day 7,
candle

each egg in a dark room with a torch pressed against the large end. A live egg shows a web of blood vessels around a dark centre; an infertile or early-dead egg is clear with no development. Remove clear eggs so the hen does not waste energy on them.

After day 18, stop turning or moving the eggs. The embryo has orientated itself for hatching and moving eggs at this stage risks the chick aligning incorrectly inside the shell. The hen knows this instinctively and will shift her behaviour: she stops turning and sits lower, flatter, and more tightly.
Hatch day

Day 21 is the expected hatch date for chicken eggs. The first sign is a faint cheeping from inside the shell, followed by the pip: a small star-shaped crack appears at the large end as the chick breaks through with its egg tooth. From pip to full emergence takes 12 to 24 hours in a healthy chick.

Do not intervene unless a chick has been pipped and unmoving for more than 24 hours. Premature assistance tears blood vessels in the membrane and kills the chick. A chick that takes 30 hours to emerge is often weaker than average but viable; one that is fully absorbed and dry needs only patience.

Leave newly hatched chicks under the hen for at least 24 hours. They do not need food or water immediately; the absorbed yolk sustains them through the first day. Introduce a shallow water drinker and chick crumb once the whole clutch has hatched and dried.

items.fix: Candle the eggs to check viability, then either transfer them to an incubator or accept the loss. A broken broody rarely restarts on the same clutch.
items.cause: The broody has broken, perhaps due to disturbance, illness, or insufficient commitment from the start
items.symptom: Hen leaving the nest after day 10

items.fix: Candle all unhatched eggs in a dark room. A rocking egg contains a live late embryo; leave it under the hen for another day. A still, dark egg is a late death; remove it carefully.
items.cause: Low fertility in the hatching eggs, a temperature problem early in incubation, or the eggs were stored too long before being set

items.fix: Remove the chick to a warm brooder for 24 hours and reintroduce under the hen after dark when she has other chicks around her.
items.cause: Rare, but some first-time broodys do not immediately recognise the chick
items.symptom: Chick hatches but hen is hostile to it

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
